text: There are also populations of immune cells in our nose, which wake up when they detect high levels of sneeze-inducing compounds. “Some of the chemicals that are made as a consequence of that immune response cause changes in the lining of our nose,” says Sheena Cruickshank, a professor in the University of Manchester’s Division of Immunology. Those changes will be familiar to anyone who has endured a pollen-laden summer or phlegmy winter. The body makes more mucus, swelling starts in the nose, and signals are sent to the brain via the trigeminal nerve(三叉神经,面部感觉的主要神经), which provides sensation to the face. This signal is processed by an area at the base of our brain called the medulla oblongata(延髓,大脑的一部分，控制许多自主功能，如心跳和呼吸), resulting in reflexive muscle contractions(反射性肌肉收缩). This all leads to a sneeze. But while the causes of sneezing vary, there’s no reason a virus should produce a louder sneeze than grass pollen, says Cruickshank.

Sneezing is an important mechanism for the body, as it helps flush out particles that build up in our nose as we breathe. It occurs when immune cells detect sneeze-inducing compounds in our nose, which causes the body to produce more mucus, and for signals to be sent to the brain that result in reflexive muscle contractions, leading to a sneeze. For a sneeze to occur, the first step is for the body to take a deep breath. Our vocal cords close tightly, and once enough pressure has built up in our lungs, all the air is expelled in a sneeze. Whether or not we have a quiet or booming sneeze is influenced by the shape and “floppiness” of our vocal cords and other soft tissue at the back of the throat. Cultural expectations may also influence how loudly or quietly we sneeze.

Sneezing serves as a crucial bodily function, acting as a protective mechanism to expel accumulated particles in our nose throughout breathing. This process is activated when immune cells identify small sneeze-inducing compounds in our nasal cavity. Such detection triggers an increase in mucus production and initiates sneeze signals to the brain. These signals are processed by the hippocampus, leading to reflexive muscle contractions that determine the speed of the sneeze.

The sneezing process begins with the body drawing a deep breath. Our vocal cords then clamp shut, and once sufficient pressure accumulates in our lungs, it is released in a powerful sneeze. The volume of a sneeze, whether it's subtle or resounding, is influenced by the structure and laxity of our vocal cords and other soft tissues at the back of the throat. Additionally, cultural factors, psychological factors, and societal norms can also influence the sound of a sneeze.

It's essential to note that holding back a sneeze may damage the surface of the nose and sinus. Therefore, the most effective way to sneeze quietly is by keeping the mouth closed. This sneeze gene-driven process not only helps flush out old cells but also aids the immune system in protecting from viruses.
